meta.notes: SPaG concepts using direct sentence selection (SAT-aligned, token-based)

topics.label: Nouns
topics.definition: A noun is a naming word for a person, place, thing or idea.

examples.sentence: The dog ran across the field.
examples.explanation: Dog and field are nouns because they name things.

questions.sentence: The cat slept on the sofa.
questions.task: Select all the nouns in the sentence.
Select all the nouns in the sentence.
Select the noun in the sentence.
Select all the nouns in the sentence.
Select the noun in the sentence.

topics.label: Verbs
topics.definition: A verb shows an action or a state of being. Some verbs are auxiliary (helping) verbs.

examples.explanation: Is is an auxiliary verb and running is the main verb.

questions.task: Select the verb in the sentence.
Select the verb in the sentence.
Select all the verbs, including auxiliary verbs.
Select the verb in the sentence.
Select the verb in the sentence.
Select all the verbs, including auxiliary verbs.

questions.sentence: They are going to the park.
questions.task: Select all the verbs, including auxiliary verbs.

questions.sentence: He did not understand the question.
questions.task: Select all the verbs, including auxiliary verbs.
Select all the verbs, including modal verbs.

examples.sentence: The tall building touched the clouds.

questions.task: Select the adjective in the sentence.
Select the adjective in the sentence.
Select the adjective in the sentence.
Select the adjective in the sentence.
Select all the adjectives in the sentence.

topics.label: Adverbs
topics.definition: An adverb gives more information about a verb. It often tells us how, when or where.

examples.sentence: She ran quickly to the gate.
examples.explanation: Quickly tells us how she ran.

questions.sentence: He spoke softly to the baby.
questions.task: Select the adverb in the sentence.
Select the adverb in the sentence.
Select the adverb in the sentence.
Select the adverb in the sentence.
Select all the adverbs in the sentence.

topics.label: Determiners
topics.definition: A determiner comes before a noun and shows which one or how many.

questions.task: Select all the determiners in the sentence.

questions.sentence: The cat sat on the mat.
questions.task: Select all the determiners in the sentence.
Select all the determiners in the sentence.
Select the determiner in the sentence.
Select the determiner in the sentence.

topics.label: Prepositions
topics.definition: A preposition shows the relationship between a noun and another word in the sentence.

examples.sentence: The cat slept under the table.
examples.explanation: Under shows the position of the cat.

questions.sentence: The book is on the shelf.
questions.task: Select the preposition in the sentence.
Select the preposition in the sentence.
Select the preposition in the sentence.

questions.sentence: The keys are inside the drawer.
questions.task: Select the preposition in the sentence.
Select the preposition in the sentence.

topics.label: Subordinate Clauses
topics.definition: A subordinate clause gives extra information but cannot stand alone as a sentence.

examples.sentence: I stayed inside because it was raining.
examples.explanation: Because it was raining is a subordinate clause.

questions.sentence: She stayed home because she felt ill.
questions.task: Select the subordinate clause in the sentence.

questions.sentence: When the bell rang, the class left.
questions.task: Select the subordinate clause in the sentence.

questions.sentence: I will go if it stops raining.
questions.task: Select the subordinate clause in the sentence.

questions.sentence: Although he was tired, he continued.
questions.task: Select the subordinate clause in the sentence.

questions.sentence: We waited while the rain fell.
questions.task: Select the subordinate clause in the sentence.

topics.label: Pronouns
topics.definition: A pronoun replaces a noun to avoid repetition.

examples.explanation: Her is a pronoun referring to Sarah.

questions.task: Select the pronoun in the sentence.
Select the pronoun in the sentence.
Select all the pronouns in the sentence.
Select the pronoun in the sentence.

questions.sentence: This is the one I like.
questions.task: Select all the pronouns in the sentence.

topics.label: Conjunctions
topics.definition: A conjunction joins words, phrases or clauses.

examples.sentence: I wanted to go but it was raining.

questions.task: Select the conjunction in the sentence.

questions.sentence: I stayed inside because it was cold.
questions.task: Select the conjunction in the sentence.
Select the conjunction in the sentence.
Select the conjunction in the sentence.

questions.sentence: You can go if you finish.
questions.task: Select the conjunction in the sentence.

topics.label: Active Voice
topics.definition: In an active sentence the subject performs the action. The verb is a straightforward doing word: The dog bit the postman. The dog (subject) does the biting.

examples.explanation: Bit is the active verb — the dog (subject) performs the action.

topics.label: Passive Voice
topics.definition: In a passive sentence the subject receives the action. We form the passive with was/were + past participle. The doer can be added with by: The postman was bitten by the dog.

examples.sentence: The postman was bitten by the dog.
examples.explanation: Was bitten is the passive verb phrase — the postman (subject) receives the action. The dog follows 'by'.

questions.sentence: The postman was bitten by the dog.

parts.label: Subject
parts.hint: who or what the sentence is about — it receives the action

parts.label: Passive verb
parts.hint: the auxiliary verb and past participle

parts.label: Agent
parts.hint: who actually does the action — includes 'by'

topics.label: Simple Present
topics.definition: The simple present tense describes actions happening now, regularly, or as a general truth. Regular verbs add -s or -es in the third person singular (e.g. runs, watches).

examples.sentence: She runs to school every morning.
examples.explanation: Runs is a simple present tense verb — it describes a regular, repeated action.

questions.task: Select the simple present tense verb.
Select the simple present tense verb.

questions.sentence: The sun rises in the east.
questions.task: Select the simple present tense verb.
Select the simple present tense verb.
Select the simple present tense verb.

topics.label: Simple Past
topics.definition: The simple past tense describes a completed action in the past. Regular verbs add -ed (e.g. walked). Many common verbs are irregular (e.g. ran, ate, broke, wrote).

examples.sentence: She ran to school this morning.
examples.explanation: Ran is a simple past tense verb — it describes a completed action. Ran is the irregular past of run.

questions.task: Select the simple past tense verb.
Select the simple past tense verb.
Select the simple past tense verb.
Select the simple past tense verb.
Select the simple past tense verb.

topics.label: Present Progressive
topics.definition: The present progressive (or present continuous) describes actions happening right now or around the present time. It is formed with am/is/are + verb + -ing. Example: I am playing.

examples.explanation: "Is writing" is the present progressive verb phrase — "is" (auxiliary) + "writing" (main verb + -ing).

questions.sentence: She is dancing in the hall.
questions.task: Select all parts of the present progressive verb phrase.
Select all parts of the present progressive verb phrase.
Select all parts of the present progressive verb phrase.

questions.sentence: The dog is sleeping on the sofa.
questions.task: Select all parts of the present progressive verb phrase.
Select all parts of the present progressive verb phrase.

topics.label: Past Progressive
topics.definition: The past progressive (or past continuous) describes actions that were in progress at a particular point in the past. It is formed with was/were + verb + -ing. Example: I was playing.

examples.sentence: She was singing when the bell rang.
examples.explanation: "Was singing" is the past progressive verb phrase — "was" (auxiliary) + "singing" (main verb + -ing).

questions.sentence: He was reading when I arrived.
questions.task: Select all parts of the past progressive verb phrase.

questions.sentence: They were playing football in the rain.
questions.task: Select all parts of the past progressive verb phrase.

questions.sentence: I was cooking dinner when the phone rang.
questions.task: Select all parts of the past progressive verb phrase.

questions.sentence: The children were laughing at the clown.
questions.task: Select all parts of the past progressive verb phrase.

questions.sentence: She was running to catch the bus.
questions.task: Select all parts of the past progressive verb phrase.

topics.label: Present Perfect
topics.definition: The present perfect is formed with has or have + past participle. It links the past to the present — describing something that just happened, or that started in the past and still matters now.

examples.explanation: Has won is a present perfect verb phrase — has is the auxiliary verb and won is the past participle.

questions.task: Select the present perfect verb phrase (has/have + past participle).

questions.sentence: They have arrived at the station.
questions.task: Select the present perfect verb phrase (has/have + past participle).
Select the present perfect verb phrase (has/have + past participle).

questions.sentence: The children have eaten their lunch.
questions.task: Select the present perfect verb phrase (has/have + past participle).

questions.sentence: The goalkeeper has saved five penalties.
questions.task: Select the present perfect verb phrase (has/have + past participle).

topics.label: Past Perfect
topics.definition: The past perfect is formed with had + past participle. It describes an action that was completed before another event in the past — the further-back past.

examples.sentence: By the time he arrived, she had won the race.
examples.explanation: Had won is past perfect — her winning happened before he arrived. We use had + past participle to show this earlier past event.

questions.sentence: She had finished her homework before dinner.
questions.task: Select the past perfect verb phrase (had + past participle).

questions.sentence: They had left before the storm arrived.
questions.task: Select the past perfect verb phrase (had + past participle).
Select the past perfect verb phrase (had + past participle).

questions.sentence: By the time we arrived, the shop had closed.
questions.task: Select the past perfect verb phrase (had + past participle).

questions.sentence: She had read the book three times.
questions.task: Select the past perfect verb phrase (had + past participle).

topics.label: Simple Future
topics.definition: The simple future describes actions that will happen after now. It is formed with will + base verb. Example: I will play. It can also be expressed with am/is/are going to + base verb.

examples.sentence: She will visit her grandparents tomorrow.
examples.explanation: "Will visit" is the simple future — "will" (modal auxiliary) + "visit" (base form of the verb, no -ing or -ed).

questions.sentence: He will start his new school next week.
questions.task: Select all parts of the simple future verb phrase.

questions.sentence: They will travel to France in the summer.
questions.task: Select all parts of the simple future verb phrase.

questions.sentence: I will finish my homework after dinner.
questions.task: Select all parts of the simple future verb phrase.

questions.sentence: The sun will rise at six tomorrow morning.
questions.task: Select all parts of the simple future verb phrase.

questions.sentence: We will celebrate after the test.
questions.task: Select all parts of the simple future verb phrase.
